talk_title: Obesity as a modifiable risk factor for cancer incidence and outcomes

models.name: International Agency for Research on Cancer meta‑analysis (≈1,000 studies)
Swedish Obese Subjects study (≈2,000 bariatric vs 2,000 controls, 25‑yr follow‑up)
Oxford Overview Group early‑stage breast cancer recurrence analysis (n≈200,000)
Look AHEAD lifestyle trial (n≈700 incident cancers, 11‑yr follow‑up)

clinical_data.metric: breast cancer risk per 5 kg/m² BMI increase
cancer‑specific mortality risk per 5 kg/m² BMI increase (breast cancer)
overall cancer incidence after bariatric surgery
obesity‑related cancer incidence after bariatric surgery
clinical_data.value: ≈50% lower breast cancer risk, ≈60% lower colorectal cancer risk

clinical_data.metric: weight loss in Breast Cancer Weight Loss Trial (intervention vs control)
percentage of participants achieving ≥5% weight loss

MOA_summary: Excess adiposity drives systemic metabolic dysregulation (hyperinsulinemia, hyperleptinemia, inflammation) that increases incidence and worsens outcomes across multiple malignancies; intentional weight loss via bariatric surgery or structured lifestyle programs reverses these metabolic perturbations and reduces cancer risk and recurrence, particularly in breast cancer, by lowering insulin, leptin, and inflammatory markers.
key_findings: Each 5 kg/m² increase in BMI raises post‑menopausal breast cancer risk by ~10% (IARC meta‑analysis).
Obesity at cancer diagnosis is associated with >20% higher breast cancer‑specific mortality across 36 studies.
Bariatric surgery reduces overall cancer incidence by ~40% and obesity‑related cancers (breast ~50% reduction, colorectal ~60% reduction).
In the Breast Cancer Weight Loss Trial, the lifestyle intervention achieved a mean 6% weight loss at 1 yr, with 50% of participants losing ≥5% body weight versus 12% in controls.
Weight loss lowered fasting insulin by 21% and reduced HOMA‑IR by 25% at 6 mo, indicating reversal of insulin resistance.
Metabolic improvements (insulin, leptin, CRP) were observed without yet having invasive disease‑free survival data, which are pending.
open_questions: Will randomized weight‑loss trials demonstrate a causal reduction in cancer incidence and mortality comparable to bariatric surgery?
Which metabolic biomarkers (e.g., insulin vs leptin) are most predictive of cancer risk reduction after weight loss?
Can body‑composition metrics (DEXA, bioimpedance) improve risk stratification beyond BMI?

cited_papers: International Agency for Research on Cancer monograph (2022)
Oxford Overview Group breast cancer recurrence analysis (2023)
Look AHEAD trial secondary cancer outcomes (2024)

talk_title: Preclinical mechanisms of GLP‑1/GIP dual agonist tirzepatide in obesity‑driven breast, colon and endometrial cancer

speaker.name: Steve Hursting
speaker.affiliation: Lineberger Comprehensive Cancer Center, UNC Chapel Hill

targets.dependency_context: obese mouse models (C57BL/6) with MC38, EO771, PY230 tumors

biomarkers.name: fasting insulin
biomarkers.finding: obesity‑induced 50% increase reversed to control levels by tirzepatide

biomarkers.name: leptin
biomarkers.finding: ≈20‑fold elevation in obese mice reduced to baseline by tirzepatide

biomarkers.name: CD8+ T‑cell activation markers (granzyme B, PD‑1, TIM‑3)
biomarkers.finding: obesity‑induced naive phenotype (TCF7, CCR7) restored to activated phenotype by tirzepatide

biomarkers.name: antigen presentation gene set score
biomarkers.finding: suppressed in obese tumors (18/18 gene sets) rescued by tirzepatide

models.name: LKB1/p53‑deficient endometrial cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: obese vs lean, tumor initiation and progression arms

MOA_summary: Tirzepatide induces ~20% body‑weight loss in diet‑induced obese mice, normalizing hyperglycemia, hyperinsulinemia and hyperleptinemia; this metabolic reprogramming restores CD8⁺ T‑cell activation and antigen‑presenting cell function, reverses obesity‑driven immunosuppression, and consequently reduces tumor growth in breast, colon and endometrial cancer models, an effect that is abolished by CD8⁺ T‑cell depletion, indicating a T‑cell‑dependent mechanism.
key_findings: Tirzepatide achieved ~20% weight loss in obese C57BL/6 mice, matching human clinical trial outcomes.

Obesity‑driven tumor growth in EO771, MC38 and PY230 models was reduced to lean‑control levels by tirzepatide; pair‑feeding reproduced most of the effect, indicating weight‑loss as the primary driver.
In the LKB1/p53‑deficient endometrial model, tirzepatide reduced tumor burden even in non‑obese mice, suggesting additional metabolic effects.

Obesity suppressed CD8⁺ T‑cell activation (naive markers TCF7, CCR7) and antigen‑presentation gene sets (18/18); tirzepatide fully restored activated phenotypes (granzyme B, PD‑1) and antigen‑presentation scores.
CD8⁺ T‑cell depletion abolished tirzepatide’s anti‑tumor effect, confirming T‑cell dependence.
Tirzepatide did not affect cancer cell proliferation in vitro, consistent with low GLP‑1R/GIPR expression in human cancer cell lines.
Gut microbiome analysis showed tirzepatide enriched butyrate‑producing bacteria, potentially contributing to reduced inflammation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No direct cytotoxic effect of tirzepatide on cancer cells observed across a wide concentration range.
Effectiveness appears largely mediated by systemic metabolic improvement rather than tumor‑intrinsic target engagement.

combination_strategies.rationale: positive control for weight‑loss–mediated anti‑tumor effects
combination_strategies.phenotype: near‑complete tumor regression, superior to tirzepatide alone
tumor suppression comparable to tirzepatide, indicating weight‑loss as main driver

open_questions: Which specific metabolic factor (insulin, leptin, IGF‑1) is most critical for the immunologic rescue?
Can tirzepatide synergize with checkpoint inhibitors in obese tumor models?
Does the microbiome shift contribute causally to the restored anti‑tumor immunity?

cited_papers: Hursting et al. Nat Commun preprint 2024

talk_title: Real‑world evidence of GLP‑1 receptor agonists on cancer risk, incidence, and survivorship

models.name: Medicare 15% random sample (2016‑2020)
models.genotype_or_key_features: type 2 diabetes patients, n≈86,000 GLP‑1 vs SGLT2; n≈180,000 GLP‑1 vs DPP4

models.name: OneFlorida PCORnet EHR cohort (2014‑2024)
models.genotype_or_key_features: obesity patients with/without type 2 diabetes, n≈43,000 GLP‑1 users vs non‑users

models.name: Medicare cancer survivorship sub‑cohort (5% sample)
models.genotype_or_key_features: cancer survivors with type 2 diabetes, n≈2,500

clinical_data.metric: overall cancer incidence (GLP‑1 vs non‑users, EHR cohort)
kidney cancer incidence (GLP‑1 vs SGLT2, Medicare)
endometrial cancer incidence (GLP‑1 vs DPP4, Medicare)
overall survival among cancer survivors (GLP‑1 vs DPP4, Medicare)
breast cancer‑specific survival (GLP‑1 vs DPP4, Medicare)

MOA_summary: Observational analyses of large Medicare and EHR datasets suggest that GLP‑1 receptor agonist exposure is associated with modest reductions in overall and obesity‑related cancer incidence (notably endometrial, ovarian, meningioma) and improved survival among cancer survivors, while a potential increased risk of kidney cancer emerges in type 2 diabetes cohorts, highlighting drug‑specific effects that may be mediated by weight loss, metabolic improvement, and immune modulation observed in preclinical studies.
readouts: comparative effectiveness vs SGLT2 and DPP4
key_findings: GLP‑1 users had a 17% lower overall cancer incidence (HR 0.83) compared with matched non‑users in the OneFlorida cohort.
Significant risk reductions were observed for endometrial (HR 0.71), ovarian (HR 0.74) and meningioma (HR 0.68) cancers.
In Medicare type 2 diabetes patients, GLP‑1 vs SGLT2 was associated with a 22% increased kidney cancer risk (HR 1.22).
GLP‑1 vs DPP4 showed a borderline 18% increased endometrial cancer risk (HR 1.18) but was not statistically definitive.
Among cancer survivors with diabetes, GLP‑1 exposure improved overall survival (HR 0.78) and breast cancer‑specific survival (HR 0.71) versus DPP4.
Protective effects were consistent across age groups and most pronounced in non‑Hispanic White patients.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential increased kidney cancer risk in diabetic patients may reflect underlying CKD susceptibility amplified by GLP‑1 therapy.
open_questions: What biological mechanisms underlie the observed kidney cancer signal in diabetic cohorts?
Do newer GLP‑1 agents (semaglutide, tirzepatide) confer stronger protective effects than older agents?
How do GLP‑1 effects differ between primary prevention (risk) and secondary prevention (survivorship) settings?
